idea-plugin.name: TCP/HTTP Monitor
idea-plugin.description:
TCP/HTTP Monitor

A lightweight TCP/HTTP monitoring tool integrated directly into your IDE. Capture, observe, and debug network traffic without leaving your development environment.

Key Features

  • TCP Monitor – Forward raw TCP connections with full byte-level logging, ideal for debugging database, messaging, or custom binary protocols.
  • HTTP Monitor – Intercept HTTP requests and responses with detailed header and payload inspection, including Host header rewriting and Location header rewriting support.
  • TLS/SSL Support – Enable TLS on the incoming side, the target side, or both, with configurable certificate trust options.
  • Multi-Tab Management – Run multiple monitor instances simultaneously, each in its own tab with independent configuration and logs.
  • Connection Filtering – Quickly filter connections by keyword to locate the traffic you care about.
  • Persistent Configuration – Save monitor configurations to disk so they are automatically restored when you reopen the IDE.
  • Custom Encoding – Configure the character encoding used to display TCP payloads for human-readable output.

Use Cases

  • Debug API calls between a frontend and backend service
  • Expose services running on remote hosts to a local port for inspection
  • Inspect TLS-encrypted traffic from a target service by trusting its certificate, then expose the decrypted traffic on a local TLS or non-TLS port for debugging

Getting Started

Open the Monitor tool window from the bottom toolbar of the IDE, then click the + button to create a new monitor configuration.

Basic Configuration

  • Monitor Type – Select TCP or HTTP.
  • Local Port – The port the monitor listens on (e.g. 8080).
  • Remote Host – The target server hostname or IP (e.g. 192.168.1.100).
  • Remote Port – The target server port (e.g. 3306).
  • Encoding – Character encoding used to display TCP payloads (default: system charset).

Using TCP Monitor

  1. Select TCP as the monitor type.
  2. Enter the local port to listen on and the remote host/port to forward to.
  3. Optionally set the Encoding field (e.g. UTF-8, GBK) to decode raw bytes into readable text.
  4. Click OK to start the monitor. All raw byte traffic between the client and the target will be captured and displayed in the log panel.

Example: Forward local port 3307 to a MySQL server at 192.168.1.100:3306 to inspect the wire protocol.

Using HTTP Monitor

  1. Select HTTP as the monitor type.
  2. Enter the local port and the remote host/port of the target HTTP service.
  3. Enable Replace Host header with target to rewrite the Host request header to match the target server, avoiding virtual-host mismatches.
  4. Enable Rewrite Location header to localhost to rewrite Location response headers so redirects point back to the monitor, keeping traffic flowing through it.
  5. Click OK to start. HTTP requests and responses are logged with method, URI, status code, headers, and payload.

Example: Forward local port 8080 to an API server at api.example.com:443 with TLS enabled on the target side.

TLS Configuration

TLS options apply to both TCP and HTTP monitor types:

  • Enable TLS for incoming requests – The monitor accepts TLS-encrypted connections from clients. A built-in keystore is used for the server certificate.
  • Enable TLS for target server – The monitor connects to the target server over TLS. This is required when the target service uses HTTPS or any TLS-wrapped protocol.
  • Trust target server certificate – When enabled, the monitor accepts the target server's certificate without validation (useful for self-signed certificates in development environments). This option is only available when Enable TLS for target server is checked.

Example: To forward HTTPS traffic to a local dev server with a self-signed cert, enable both Enable TLS for target server and Trust target server certificate.

Saving and Managing Configurations

  • Click Save in the dialog to persist the configuration. Saved monitors are restored automatically on the next IDE startup (in a stopped state).
  • Use the Edit (ℹ) button to modify an existing monitor. Click Unsave to remove it from persistent storage while keeping the current tab open.
  • Use the ▶ / ■ buttons to start and stop individual monitors, and the 🗑 button to clear the connection log.
